texto: Practice giving things away, not just things you don't care about, but things you do like. Remember, it is not the size of a gift, it is its quality and the amount of mental attachment you overcome that count. So don't bankrupt yourself on a momentary positive impulse, only to regret it later. Give thought to giving. Give small things, carefully, and observe the mental processes going along with the act of releasing the little thing you liked. (53)(Quote is actually Robert A F Thurman but Huston Smith, who only wrote the introduction to my edition, seems to be given full credit for this text.)
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Huston Smith: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “Practice giving things away, not just things you don't care about, but things you do like. Rememb…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que superacao, attachment, generosity pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de generosity e gift.

texto: Never during its pilgrimage is the human spirit completely adrift and alone. From start to finish its nucleus is the Atman, the god-within... underlying its whirlpool of transient feelings, emotions, and delusions is the self-luminous, abiding point of the transpersonal god. As the sun lights the world even when cloud-covered, “the Immutable is never seen but is the Witness; it is never heard but is the Hearer; it is never thought but is the Thinker; it is never known but is the Knower. There is no other witness but This, no other knower but This." from the Upanishad
explicacao: Citação de Huston Smith sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. “Never during its pilgrimage is the human spirit completely adrift and alone. From start to finish…” articula fe, religion, spirituality em registro de fe, com relevância para contextos como spirituality e upanishads.
